title: Strengthening the Transatlantic Relationship
summary: The Harvard Kennedy School created the Project on Europe and the Transatlantic Relationship to deepen scholarship and teaching on modern Europe and to facilitate the flow of people and ideas across the Atlantic. This project permits our faculty, students and fellows to focus in seminars, public events and research projects on member states of NATO and the European Union, the United Kingdom and Canada, Russia, Ukraine, and states in Eurasia.
body: The Harvard Kennedy School created the Project on Europe and the Transatlantic Relationship to deepen scholarship and teaching on modern Europe and to facilitate the flow of people and ideas across the Atlantic. This project permits our faculty, students and fellows to focus in seminars, public events and research projects on member states of NATO and the European Union, the United Kingdom and Canada, Russia, Ukraine, and states in Eurasia. We have deepened our academic commitment to understanding Europe through the Pierre Keller Visiting Professorship, which brings a preeminent European academic or policy practitioner to teach issues of contemporary public policy at Harvard Kennedy School. Similarly, with an expanded fellowship and seminar program, and a conference series in partnership with leading think tanks in Europe, we ensure that the next generation of transatlantic leaders are able to advance solutions to the most pressing issues of our time. When we launched the Project in 2018, the 75-year old alliance was in crisis. Although Europe remained our largest trading partner and the largest investor in the American economy, there was a profound lack of trust and cohesion between the U.S. and its European partners. In response to divisions and challenges facing the NATO Alliance, we published our first major report in February 2019: “ NATO at Seventy: An Alliance in Crisis ” which I co-authored with Senior Fellow, Ambassador Doug Lute. The report outlined ten challenges and recommendations to strengthen the world’s oldest and most successful military alliance for the decades ahead. We argued that the absence of strong, principled American presidential leadership was NATO’s single greatest challenge. The United States, Europe and Canada must work together toward one ambition —to renew, revitalize and retool for the decade ahead the most powerful democratic community in modern history. To that end, Harvard Kennedy School joined with the German Council on Foreign Relations (DGAP) to develop new ideas and strategies for reimagining the transatlantic relationship in 2021 and beyond. Dr. Daniela Schwarzer, Director of DGAP and I co-chaired a transatlantic strategy group comprised of experts and former government officials from the U.S. and Europe. Dr. Torrey Taussig, Research Director for the Project on Europe, led our research efforts. The result was a December 2020 report, “Stronger Together: A Strategy to Revitalize Transatlantic Power” that included eight individual action plans with policy recommendations on how to address critical challenges, such as a more assertive China and Russia, climate change, A.I. and emerging technologies, and a global trading system in flux, among other issues. The Project on Europe and the Transatlantic Relationship is committed to advancing strong ties across the Atlantic, the resurgence of democracy and human freedom, and enhancing our understanding of modern Europe at the Harvard Kennedy School. result.main_content_en: The Harvard Kennedy School's Project on Europe and the Transatlantic Relationship was established to deepen academic focus on modern Europe and foster the exchange of people and ideas across the Atlantic. It facilitates seminars, research, and public events concerning NATO, the EU, the UK, Canada, Russia, Ukraine, and Eurasia. Key initiatives include the Pierre Keller Visiting Professorship and expanded fellowship programs. In response to a crisis of trust within the transatlantic alliance, the project published the report 'NATO at Seventy: An Alliance in Crisis' in 2019, identifying challenges and recommending renewal. Subsequently, in partnership with the German Council on Foreign Relations (DGAP), it produced the 2020 report 'Stronger Together: A Strategy to Revitalize Transatlantic Power.' This report offers action plans to address pressing issues like a rising China, Russian assertiveness, climate change, AI, emerging technologies, and global trade instability. The project remains dedicated to strengthening transatlantic ties, supporting democracy, and enhancing the study of modern Europe.
result.main_content_zh: 哈佛大学肯尼迪学院设立“欧洲与跨大西洋关系项目”，旨在深化对现代欧洲的学术研究，并促进跨大西洋的人员与思想交流。该项目通过研讨会、研究和公共活动，聚焦北约、欧盟、英国、加拿大、俄罗斯、乌克兰及欧亚地区国家。核心举措包括设立皮埃尔·凯勒客座教授职位以及扩展研究员和研讨会项目。针对跨大西洋联盟内部出现的信任危机，该项目于2019年发布了报告《七十岁的北约：危机中的联盟》，指出了联盟面临的挑战并提出了振兴建议。随后，项目与德国外交关系协会（DGAP）合作，于2020年共同发布了报告《更强大的在一起：重振跨大西洋实力的战略》。该报告提供了八项具体行动计划，旨在应对中国崛起、俄罗斯的强势行为、气候变化、人工智能与新兴技术、全球贸易体系动荡等关键挑战。该项目持续致力于加强跨大西洋联系、支持民主复兴，并深化对现代欧洲的理解。

keywords_zh: 欧洲与跨大西洋关系项目
